data.title: Northern Multi-Manager Global Listed Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
In the third quarter of 2025, the global listed infrastructure sector, as measured by the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, returned 3.50%. While utilities and airports were the top performing sectors, highways and rail tracks and independent power producers (IPPs) lagged during the quarter. From a regional perspective, Canada, Western Europe and the United States outperformed while the United Kingdom and Australia lagged.

The Fund underperformed its benchmark, the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, during the third quarter with a return of 2.60%. The primary drivers of the underperformance was an overweight to the United Kingdom, an underweight to and stock selection in Canada, and non-benchmark positioning in communications towers. Contributors to performance included stock selection in the United States, including non-benchmark exposures to U.S. railroads, and stock selection in highways and rail tracks. Sub-adviser Lazard (LAZ) underperformed due to an overweight in the United Kingdom and an underweight to Canada. Sub-adviser First Sentier Investors outperformed on positive stock selection in utilities and out of benchmark holdings in U.S. railroads. Sub-adviser Cohen & Steers (CNS) outperformed due to positive stock selection in utilities and oil and gas infrastructure. Sub-adviser KBI Global Investors underperformed as renewable utilities lagged. Sub-adviser Eagle Global Advisors underperformed on poor stock selection in oil and gas infrastructure.

Concentration Risk: Investing a high percentage of net assets in securities in a specific industry, the Fund may be subject to greater volatility than a fund that is more broadly diversified.

Currency Risk: Foreign currencies will fluctuate in value relative to the U.S. dollar; therefore you may lose money if the local currency of a foreign market depreciates against the U.S. dollar.

Emerging and Frontier Markets Risk: Emerging and frontier market investing may be subject to additional economic, political, liquidity and currency risks not associated with more developed countries. Additionally, frontier countries generally have smaller economies or less developed capital markets than traditional emerging markets and, as a result, the risks of investing in emerging market countries are magnified in frontier countries.

Equity Risk: Equity securities (stocks) are more volatile and carry more risk than other forms of investments, including investments in high-grade fixed-income securities. The net asset value per share of this Fund will fluctuate as the value of the securities in the portfolio changes.

Foreign (Non-U.S.) Securities Risk: Investing in foreign (non-U.S.) securities may result in the Fund experiencing more rapid and extreme changes in value than a fund that invests exclusively in securities of U.S. companies, due to less liquid markets, foreign government intervention and adverse economic, political, diplomatic, financial and regulatory factors.

Infrastructure Companies Risk: Infrastructure companies may not realize projected revenue volumes due to; cost overruns; changes in terms making a project no longer economical; macroeconomic factors may raise the average cost of funding; government regulation; government budgetary constraints; special tariffs and/or changes in tax law and unfavorable accounting standards.

Liquidity Risk: Some securities held by the Fund may be difficult to sell, or illiquid, particularly during times of market turmoil. Illiquid securities also may be difficult to value. If the Fund is forced to sell an illiquid asset to meet redemption requests or other cash needs, the Fund may be forced to sell at a loss.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) Risk: Investing in MLPs involves certain risks related to investing in the underlying assets of the MLPs and risks associated with pooled investment vehicles. MLPs that concentrate in a particular industry or a particular geographic region are subject to risks associated with such industry or region. The benefit derived from the Fund's investment in MLPs is largely dependent on the MLPs being treated as partnerships for federal income tax purposes, treatment of an MLP (owned by the Fund) as a corporation would materially reduce the after-tax return to the Fund with respect to its investment in the MLP. The Fund must include its allocable share of the MLP's taxable income in its taxable income, whether or not it receives a distribution of cash from the MLP. In such cases, the Fund may have to liquidate securities to make required distributions to the Fund's shareholders.

Mid-Cap Risk: Mid-capitalization stocks typically carry additional risk, since smaller companies generally have higher risk of failure and, historically, their stocks have experienced a greater degree of volatility.

Multi-Manager Risk: is the risk that the sub-advisers' investment styles will not always be complementary, which could affect the performance of the Fund.

Non-Diversified Risk: The Fund invests in a smaller number of securities than the average mutual fund. The change in value of a single holding may have a more pronounced effect on the Fund's net asset value and performance than for other funds.

Small-Cap Risk: Small-capitalization funds typically carry additional risks since smaller companies generally have a higher risk of failure. Their stocks are subject to a greater degree of volatility, trade in lower volume and may be less liquid.

data.title: IAUI: A Hold For 2026 As Gold Rally Slows
data.description:
High-yield income funds are all in vogue now, and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) is a new addition this year. IAUI offers exposure to gold and a monthly distribution averaging $0.54 since inception. This adds up to an annualized yield of around 12%.

A common feature of income funds is that the options overlay strategy caps gains. This can be a major disadvantage during strong rallies like gold (IAU) had from August to October. However, the strategy shines during slower consolidation phases, and I think gold is in one now. This article looks at why this could be a good time to hold IAUI.

IAUI Basics

IAUI was launched by NEOS in June 2025. NEOS tends to find a reasonable balance of capital appreciation and income in its income ETFs and won an award for best options strategies ETF issuer.

The fund seeks to generate high monthly income through selling covered calls. This premium is then distributed monthly. A fairly steady distribution is paid out, and since gold has appreciated, so has the payment.

Note that because IAUI has only paid out for half a year, its currently listed TTM yield is half. After a full 12 months, it should be around 12-13%

Distributions are partially classed as return of capital for tax reasons, and the fund page states,

Distributions made by the Fund have been classified as a return of capital and may be comprised of option premiums, dividends, capital gains, and interest payments. As of the most recent distribution by the Fund, 92% was estimated to be return of capital. Please see the 19a-1 notices for a more comprehensive breakdown.

Note, the ROC is not eroding the NAV. The fund can declare a loss on the options even though they are not strictly a loss, as they are covered by gains in the underlying holdings.

When possible, they [management] seek to find losses in each ETF’s portfolio that can be used to offset gains, potentially helping a portion of Fund distributions receive a return of capital designation.

IAUI also provides the potential for capital appreciation based on exposure to exchange-traded products (“ETPs”) that have direct exposure to gold. The upside is capped due to the options, but that is a feature of all covered call strategies. Its holdings list looks like this -

The treasuries are just a cash alternative and collateral for the derivatives.

Since IAUI is actively managed, the options can be traded and changed so that a favorable income/capital appreciation balance is achieved. The expense fee is 0.78%, which is average for active management and average compared to its peers.

While these are all income funds based on gold, there are some major differences in yields and structure. Some have extremely high yields but less participation in rallies, while others are at the other end of the spectrum. Making a performance comparison may not be fair, as IAUI has yet to trade for 6 months and has yet to be tested under all market conditions.

IAUI's closest peers are GLDI and IGLD, and it's encouraging to see its total return compare favorably. GLDI and IGLD have been trading since 2021, so they have a proven track record.

Outperforming Gold

There's a misconception that income funds always underperform the underlying asset, but that really depends on the structure of the fund and the price action of the underlying.

Gold tends to experience long consolidation phases followed by strong break-outs and rallies. The last resting period happened in April-August, which saw gold trade in a contracting triangle.

IAUI outperformed IAU slightly during this period.

It has also outperformed IAU since the October peak as gold appears to be forming another consolidation.

Unfortunately, volatility also falls during these consolidation periods, and that means the income from options will be lower.

.

Still, IAUI should be able to provide a slight outperformance during consolidation phases. Furthermore, the drawdowns should be less, and of course, the steady income is a major plus.

On the downside, should gold break out strongly and repeat the August-October rally, IAUI will be left behind. IAUI has underperformed IAU by 7% since inception.

I don't want to leave 7% on the table, but this is a narrower underperformance than many other funds experience, and it's something I can tolerate. In any case, I am doubtful gold will repeat 2025's performance, which has been unusually strong.

While I am bullish, I'm not that bullish. I think there will be a rest in the $3800-$4300 area for several more months, in which time I would much rather hold IAUI than IAU.

Risks

IAUI is a new fund and does not have a proven track record. It may underperform similar ETFs in the longer term.

IAUI has not traded under a gold correction or bear market. In theory, it should slightly outperform during drawdowns, and the distributions should remain fairly constant, but that is not a guarantee.

Conclusions

IAUI has a good balance between high-income and capital appreciation and can outperform IAU during consolidation phases and minor drawdowns.

I think IAUI could be a good choice into early 2026, as gold looks to be entering an extended sideways period to consolidate the strong 2025 gains. If and when it does rally again, IAUI will still provide exposure and, of course, income. I rate it a buy.

data.title: Ford: Strong YTD Rally, But Future Returns Likely To Track The Market
data.description:
Ford (F) is an American automotive heavyweight that appears to be experiencing a significant improvement in the fundamentals, highlighting an accelerating revenue growth and a streak of five consecutive double-beat quarters. The stock is up 31% on a year-to-date basis and has significantly outperformed the benchmark.

The company trades at roughly 12x earnings multiple, implying a 27% discount versus peers at approximately 17x. Ford offers a 5.85% dividend yield, which seems to be an appealing bonus to an undervalued name. Although it may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking dividend income, it hasn't grown dividends over the past year, and is heavily leveraged with $164 billion in total obligations.

I think the stock may consolidate after a significant rally. The current valuation implies a multi-year high, which also signals that upside may be limited in the near term. It could appear a compelling diversification in the consumer discretionary space if bottom-line growth delivers and shareholder-friendly approach continues.

However, I wouldn't be surprised if the automotive company experiences a profit-taking after a sharp upside move, which would support my neutral stance. I rate Ford a Hold with a $13.70 price target. This presents a 5% upside possibility without dividends, and roughly market-like performance with yield. The company appears to be a well-known name in the space, but suppressed margins and heavy leverage cloud the future outlook, especially if the Fed decides to pause the easing cycle.

Turnaround Remains In Progress

On October 23, Ford delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was the fifth consecutive double-beat quarterly performance, indicating improving fundamentals and a solid market position. The market reacted to the results with a 12% upside over the following trading day.

The automotive company posted $50.5 billion in revenue, implying a nearly 10% revenue growth, and $3.32 billion above what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $0.45 in diluted EPS, a roughly 10% decline on a year-over-year basis versus $0.49 in the same quarter last year, but $0.09 above analysts' estimates. The company experienced significant revenue growth, which contributed to the improved investor confidence.

In the earnings presentation, management underscored that it has achieved $4.3 billion in adjusted free cash flow, highlighting a $1.1 billion increase on a year-over-year basis.

The company's top-line performance was driven by the segment of Model E, unlocking a new revenue growth cycle tied to EV and hybrid vehicles. Although the category achieved an EBIT loss of $1.4 billion, this remains reasonable and justified by expansion, indicating new product introduction in Europe. This may drag on profitability in the short term, but it remains a long-term tailwind for the automotive company. Given this, F appears to be more diversified, which may appear to be a compelling trait for long-term investors.

To support this, the global EV market was estimated at $1.3 trillion in 2024 and is expected to quadruple by 2030, reaching $6.5 trillion. This represents a 32.5% CAGR and validates the company's pivot to the EV space. If the company can continue growing the segment at a double-digit pace, it could significantly benefit from broader market growth.

The Ford Blue segment generated $1.5 billion in EBIT, reaching a 5.5% EBIT margin. The Ford Pro led the performance with $2 billion in EBIT contribution, resulting in 11.4%. The automotive name continues experiencing solid momentum in demand.

The Ford Blue remained the largest segment, indicating $28 billion in revenue, nearly a 7% increase on a year-over-year basis. Ford Pro posted $17.4 billion in top-line contribution, an 11% surge over the past 12 months, and the Model E category was the fastest growing with a 50% surge, reaching $1.8 billion in revenue. It seems that the automotive company is expanding across all cylinders, which is supporting a bullish stance, reflecting a strengthening moat.

Although the company's performance is impacted by tariffs, it continues to improve FCF. Management announced this was the fifth consecutive quarter, reflecting a reduction in costs, despite the tariff impact. This argues that Ford is well-managed and supports a positive outlook. The company has also announced a Q4 regular dividend of $0.15, continuing a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach.

To conclude, the automotive company remains a dominant player in the space. Ford experiences seven straight months of US sales growth, justifying a strong momentum in demand and improving results. The company's pivot to the EV landscape is gaining attention, and a 50% increase in revenue reflects a solid initial demand. Although Ford is impacted by tariffs, it continues improving FCF, and that fuels a bullish narrative.

However, the automotive company remains heavily leveraged with $164 billion in total obligations. This resulted in $1.221 billion in interest expense over the past 12 months. In addition to this, EV product expansion may drag on the profitability metrics, clouding the near-term outlook. The EV space remains highly competitive, and execution risks remain. If Ford can scale into the space successfully, the market may be willing to reward the automotive company with a higher premium, but struggling to succeed may result in a lower valuation.

I think a significant stock price rally may take a breather. I wouldn't be surprised if it consolidates until management disperses uncertainty tied to future demand and pressured margins. Ford may have unlocked a new growth cycle with the Model E segment, but the recent rally may have partially priced in future revenue growth; thus, I remain cautious.

Ford Remains Undervalued

The automotive heavyweight trades at a forward P/E of about 12x, which suggests a 27% discount versus peers at 17x. The company appears to be trading at roughly 90% undervaluation versus the average company in the S&P500 at about 23x earnings multiple.

Although this may argue for bullish continuation from undervalued levels, the chart below suggests the earnings multiple expanded by 2x since the beginning of the year, and the current ratio stands at a multi-year high. Even though the stock may appreciate further, I wouldn't be surprised if Ford consolidated after a sharp swing.

On a forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 1.04 and 0.29, respectively, the company trades at roughly a 40% discount versus peers at 1.29 and 0.89. This highlights Ford being undervalued and argues for continued bullish momentum. However, the forward EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT at 17.97 and 28.48, respectively, offset the bullish narrative and represent a 70% overvaluation at the midpoint versus sector medians of 10.15 and 15.72. This supports my neutral stance, highlighting a mixed picture.

Over the past 12 months, Ford has grown its revenue by 3.75%, implying a 13% advantage versus peers at 3.31%. In addition to this, based on the recent quarterly performance, reflecting accelerated revenue growth, I believe the automotive company may achieve a more rapid top-line surge. This is also supported by EV and hybrid vehicle expansion.

The bottom line increased by 33% over the past 12 months, a significant outperformance versus peers at 1%. Highlighting the improving fundamentals, indicating a cost efficiency strategy and an increase in FCF, I think Ford may be able to continue growing diluted EPS further. If F sustains a solid high-single-digit growth or can deliver a double-digit performance, there is room for multiple expansion.

Furthermore, Ford operates at suppressed margins, and with the EV and hybrid pivot, may continue weighing on the profitability. The company operates at a gross profit margin (TTM) of 7.55% and a net income margin (TTM) of 2.48%. On the upside, F generated $20.43 billion in cash from operations (TTM), reflecting a 7x outperformance versus peers at $299 million.

I think Ford's valuation presents a mixed picture. It continues trading at a discounted valuation versus the sector, but the recent surge represents a multi-year premium earnings multiple. There remains a risk of profit-taking, especially if consumer spending weakens, which could result in Ford trading closer to its business typical valuation.

Nevertheless, the automotive company continues to successfully apply cost optimization strategies, resulting in FCF growth. Ford is also tied to EV tailwinds, which could drive future revenue expansion. Although it may temporarily stress profitability metrics, the broader revenue growth picture remains intact. The company's high leverage appears concerning, but with positive FCF, it could be able to continue proceeding with a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends and reducing its debt.

Wall Street aims for $1.44 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I agree with the estimate, supported by improving fundamentals and solid momentum in demand. If the economy doesn't enter a recession, F may be able to achieve a higher target than this. However, I also think it may consolidate after a double-digit surge, and earnings multiple may contract closer to business typical levels.

Therefore, if we apply the $1.44 diluted EPS estimate of FY2026 to a 9.5x earnings multiple, we arrive at my price target of $13.70. This presents a 5% upside possibility without dividends and roughly market-like performance. Although I believe the turnaround story remains in progress, the market may want to see whether the company's pivot to the EV and hybrid space succeeds. It weighs on profitability, which could impact bottom-line expansion in the following quarters, but the broader revenue growth picture remains intact. I believe, if F succeeds and continues reporting double-digit surges in sales growth, the market may be willing to reprice the stock at a higher premium. On the other hand, any uncertainty tied to outlook may cap upside in the near term.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Ford that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company is expanding its Model E segment, but it comes with execution risk. The global EV market is expected to significantly grow over the following years, but this also comes with fierce competition. US, European, and Asian companies are competing in the space, and it may be a challenging environment to gain a significant market share in the EV space.

Furthermore, the automotive industry remains capital-intensive, and this indicates a heavily leveraged capital structure, which is a concern. Although Ford can achieve improvements in FCF, investors should monitor whether the automotive brand can sustain the progress. If results weaken, I think F may contract to the business's typical earnings multiple.

I also think that after a recent rally, there may be a lack of revenue growth catalysts that could support continued price action, resulting in broader market outperformance. It appears that investors have priced in cost optimization and solid momentum in EV and hybrid demand. Unless Ford unlocks new catalysts, it appears likely to trade in a range.

On the other hand, if Ford delivers high-single-digit or double-digit revenue growth further, it remains well-positioned to benefit from strong momentum demand. The company is tied to EV-powered tailwinds, but investors should monitor whether EV and hybrid expansion will be successful.

Conclusion

I think Ford remains a compelling diversification candidate for investors seeking expanded exposure to the automotive industry. The company has an appealing brand, a long history, and offers dividend income, which may come as a compelling bonus to an undervalued name.

The company's pivot to the EV and hybrid space has been successful so far. A 50% surge in revenue on a year-over-year basis appears impressive, but investors should remain cautious due to significant competition in the space. I also think this pivot is capital-intensive and may continue pressuring profitability performance. Although the stock remains undervalued, after a recent surge, it trades at multi-year highs, which may suggest a breather and consolidation.

I think Ford remains an interesting opportunity, but I also argue it is unlikely to outperform the benchmark over the following year. I rate Ford a Hold with a $13.70 price target, representing a 5% upside possibility without dividends. 22 Wall Street analysts forecast a $12.50 price target over the next year, signaling 3% downside risk. I am feeling more bullish, as I believe F deserves a higher premium due to its strong moat, brand presence, and long history. Time will tell, but it will be interesting to see whether expectations deliver.

data.title: EZA- Cheaply Valued, And In Dazzling Form, But Don't Get Complacent
data.description:
Solid Alpha

It’s been well-documented that 2025 has been a relatively good year for emerging markets, with the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) generating ample returns of 30%, 1.5x more than global stocks. If you thought 30% returns were good enough for a diversified ETF vehicle, how should one eulogize equities from Africa’s largest economy, where the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (EZA) has generated exceptional returns of 60%, which is double what EMs in general have managed?

Interestingly enough, despite the stellar gains, EZA, even as recently as this week, is still being bandied around as one of the more promising non-US ETFs plays for FY26.

So, if EZA’s ongoing halo has managed to garner your attention, and you’d like to better understand this ETF, and explore a potential position in the same, here are a few subplots worth considering.

Lone Man Standing, Although Diversified Options Exist

Before we expand on EZA’s good and bad qualities, it is worth noting that it is the only US-listed ETF that offers focused exposure to South African equities; that wasn’t the case until November 2023, when another alternative, the Franklin FTSE South Africa ETF (FLZA), was still in existence (FLZA eventually got liquidated on the 16 th of November 2023). Of course, if investors want a bit more diversification beyond just South African equities, they could always pursue the VanEck Africa Index (AFK), where close to two-thirds of the portfolio comes from non-SA African stocks, with South African stocks still dominating.

EZA ETF Characteristics- The Good and The Bad

Nonetheless, coming back to EZA, it has been around for 23 years now, during which it has managed to amass total AUM [assets under management] of over $560M. EZA, primarily seeks to deliver returns by tracking the MSCI South Africa 25/50 Index [MS25I], a proxy of the top 85% of South Africa’s market-cap, and consists only of 27 large- and mid-cap stocks.

However, despite having the objective of covering only a narrow pool of stocks (27) of the MS25I, EZA doesn’t do a particularly great job of mirroring the performance of its tracking index.

This is reflected in the very wide tracking error readings of 14-16% across different durations. We feel risk-averse investors too ought to be wary of pursuing EZA, as the variability of its returns tends to be a lot more spread out than a typical ETF (see standard deviation and annualized volatility readings, which are well above the ETF norm).

However, one area where EZA scores very highly is on the dividend front, where you can pocket a hefty yield of over 4.6%, which is almost 200 bps better than what most ETFs yield. Note, however, that its dividend growth trajectory is quite haphazard; over a 5-year basis, dividends have contracted, but on a trailing twelve-month basis, it is growing at an exceptional pace of triple-digit-percentages

We believe that EZA can afford to dole out such lucrative yields because it is heavily dominated by two dividend-oriented sectors- materials and financials—that jointly account for over two-thirds of the portfolio. While the financials help in covering consistent dividends over the years, material stocks are generally responsible for driving lucrative but inconsistent payouts, much of which is linked to the volatile commodity cycle, which tends to go through boom-and-bust phases.

Besides that, one intriguing quirk of EZA is that its top holding (a sizeable individual weight of 14%)- Naspers Limited (OTCPK:NPSNY)—is more of a global consumer internet and technology holding company, whose fortunes are not overwhelmingly linked to the South African economy- far from it. Rather, only 15% of its overall revenue comes from the African continent as a whole, with business largely coming from Eastern Europe and Latin America.

Long-standing observers of NPSNY may also argue that much of NPSNY’s allure comes from its elevated stake (a 23.5% stake) in the Chinese tech conglomerate- Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY). However, NPSNY (through its subsidiary Prosus) is currently in the process of gradually paring down it stake in TCEHY, and using that to buy back its own stock, which may appear to be a questionable strategy of capital allocation to a fair few investors.

How Is EZA Currently Positioned Relative to Its EM Peers?

Despite EZA’s meteoric rise this year, note that EZA’s relative strength ratio vs a diversified emerging market pocket is still trading at a 9% discount to its long-term average of 1.37x.

One could make an argument for further mean reversion in favor of South African equities, as their valuations still look relatively cheap vs. EMs, particularly in light of the superior earnings growth they are set to deliver. Basically, EZA is priced at an earnings multiple of 10x, whereas EMs are almost priced at a 50% premium of close to 15x. Listed equity financial products are typically priced at a premium when their underlying holdings are poised to deliver superior growth, but that doesn’t appear to be the case here, with EMs on course to deliver long-term earnings of less than 11%, whereas EZA’s holdings are poised to deliver a superior cadence of earnings growth of over 300 bps.

Closing Thoughts - What Do EZA’s Charts Suggest?

Momentum players are currently likely taking to EZA like moths to a flame, as it is witnessing a series of higher highs and higher lows and is trading above all its key moving averages of 50, 100, and 200 on the daily chart. However, we would urge investors not to get too carried away at this juncture, as EZA has now reached a dicey zone, which has previously served as a hindrance on the long-term charts.

As you can see from the image above, we have seen three separate instances (highlighted in yellow) where EZA has approached the $65-$72 levels, only to be faced with a spike of supply in the shares, resulting in a sell-off. Note also that the price is currently perched outside the upper Bollinger band, which is a reflection of overextension, as it is two standard deviations above the 20-month rolling average. Supplementing this upward overextension, we also have the monthly RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart, which has now breached the overbought zone. The last time the RSI hit similar levels was way back in 2007, after which EZA ended up witnessing a contraction in its market-cap by roughly two-thirds, which took place over a 1-year time frame!

We are not suggesting that one should brace for a similar degree of selling pressure (particularly when EZA’s valuations and yields still look attractive), but it is certainly worth pondering if we are not too far away from an intermediate top. All in all, we don’t believe it would pay to be too bullish at this juncture, and prefer to go with a hold rating.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Market volatility persisted during the third quarter, driven by lingering tariff concerns, inflation, and geopolitical instability. Nevertheless, U.S. equity markets continued to surge, with the S&P 500 advancing over 8% during the quarter.

Equity markets continued to set record highs during the quarter, largely fueled by the artificial intelligence (AI) boom. However, gains were uneven, with weak earnings, reduced guidance, and sector rotations weighing on many names. In September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) announced its first rate cut of the year, cutting its benchmark interest rate by 0.25%, citing slowing job growth, a cooling labor market, and slowing economic growth. Looking ahead, many investors expect an additional one to two rate cuts before year-end.

Bullish investors remain focused on the strength and resilience of the U.S. economy, additional rate cuts, and the continued technological innovation driven by AI that is likely to fuel growth across numerous industries.

More bearish investors remain concerned about policy uncertainty, geopolitical instability, elevated valuations, and lingering tariff concerns risks.

Within the mid-cap space, value stocks outperformed growth stocks for the quarter (Russell Mid-cap Value Index: 6.18% versus Russell Mid-cap Growth Index: 2.78%).

Quarter

The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund outperformed its Index, the Russell Mid-Cap Value Index (the Index), on a gross basis, in the third quarter of 2025.

U.S. equity markets extended their rally after the market's swoon in early 2025 with all of the major U.S. indexes finishing firmly in positive territory. While growth stocks outperformed among large-cap stocks by a wide margin, value stocks performed better in the mid-cap and small-cap space. Indeed, the small cap Russell 2000 was the quarter's big winner by far, with both growth and value stocks advancing by more than 12%. With value stocks performing well among mid-cap stocks, the Fund outperformed during the quarter as the Fund's valuation factors skewed positively while our business prospect factors were only modestly productive.

The Fund outperformed the Index in six of the eleven sectors for the quarter.

Contributors to performance included Industrial and Real Estate stocks. The Fund benefited from strong selection among Construction & Engineering and Professional Services stocks, which generally benefited from infrastructure spending related to the development of AI development. In Real Estate, the Fund's underweight exposure to this underperforming sector coupled with good selection supported performance.

Utility and Information Technology (IT) stocks detracted from the Fund's performance. In Utilities, underperformance stemmed from poor selection and an underweight position in Electric Utilities, which generally performed well during the quarter. Among Information Technology stocks, the Fund suffered from poor selection among IT Services stocks, particularly in the Internet Services and Infrastructure industry.

OUTLOOK & POSITIONING

Investors in US equity markets can be forgiven for forgetting the ear-year rapid and sizeable correction as stocks roared back into firmly positive territory following the second and third quarter's powerful rally. After stabilizing in the first quarter, growth stocks re-established their dominance over value into the third quarter, especially among large caps. The outperformance of large-cap relative to small-cap stocks also re-emerged, although the gap in performance was not as wide as in previous years.

The market's big rally off its lows was driven largely by several key developments. The tariff threat from the Trump administration which roiled markets early in the year receded as negotiations brought many of the penalties to much more manageable levels. The Fed moved off its "wait and see" approach to interest rates and reduced its key Federal-Funds rate by 25 basis points and signaled it might make several more cuts in future periods. Spending on AI large language models continued to increase dramatically, expanding into real estate development, construction and power development. Together, these items and others encouraged significant flows into U.S. and global equity markets.

From a broader perspective, while the market's nosedive and subsequent rapid recovery have made investors breathe a bit easier, we would continue to be cautious about signaling the "all clear" on other potential risks. Earnings growth has been strong but has been limited to only a few sectors. At the same time, valuations have become even more stretched, reaching historically high levels, especially among larger cap indexes. We are also seeing some signs of growing speculative activity, with the return of "meme stocks" and outperformance by shares in companies with no or negative earnings. And while the Fed has stepped in to offset some of the weakness emerging in the jobs market, their intervention may be limited by persistent inflation. Given the risk backdrop, we continue to anticipate more moderate returns and heightened volatility in coming quarters. Within this context, we favor cheaper rather than more expensive stocks, given their still deeply discounted valuations, and we believe that the Fund is well positioned to benefit from this dynamic.

data.title: Allspring Intermediate Tax/AMT-Free Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Credit was the largest driver of performance, but duration and selection detracted.

Market review

During the third quarter, municipal returns were positive (3.00%) and outperformed taxable fixed income asset classes, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 2.51% and Treasuries 1.51% and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returning 2.03%, corporates 2.60%, and U.S. high yield 2.54%. Within municipal credit tiers, A-rated bonds performed best (3.08%) and high yield bonds were the weakest (1.63%). The yield on the U.S. Treasury 10-year note ended September at 4.16%, down 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) over the quarter and down 42 bps from the start of 2025. U.S. equity markets were positive for the quarter.

With the downward movement of the municipal curve, shorter-end municipal-to-Treasury ratios decreased during the quarter while longer ratios remained cheap. Municipal 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year ratios of 64%, 62%, and 70%, respectively, were rich, while the 30-year ratio of 90% remains the cheapest portion of the tax-exempt market relative to taxable fixed income.

Net inflows were $11 billion for the quarter, and gross issuance was approximately $157 billion (a 3% quarter-over-quarter decrease), with tax-exempt issuance of $150 billion (a 0.5% quarter-over-quarter increase).

In September, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. The municipal benchmark yield curve moved downward, with rates on the short end declining by as much as 36 bps and longer rates by as much as 30 bps. Market expectations favor at least one additional cut this year, though the size and timing of Federal Reserve action remains uncertain. The rate environment reinforces the case for municipal bonds as a stable, income-generating asset amid ongoing market volatility.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration—modest detractor

  • The fund was short duration to the benchmark during the period, which was positive early in the period but detracted in September as the market rallied.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. We expect record issuance to continue through the end of the year and to outpace fund flows, which points to a weaker technical environment for municipals. Flows remained positive through the quarter, with year-to-date inflows at $29 billion, though total inflows remain below historical averages. We remain bullish on municipals because absolute rates represent a compelling entry point, and over the near term, we expect income to be the driver for the asset class. With the recent yield curve volatility, we expect to maintain duration closer to neutral, and we see opportunities in the intermediate portion of the curve and expect to be selective about adding duration.

Yield curve—neutral

  • An underweight to bonds inside of six years was positive as this maturity range underperformed and overweights to the 8- to 12-year range and 17-year-plus range were positive, as these exposures were additive to performance. These positives were offset by underweights in 6-8 years and 12-17 years, as these maturity ranges outperformed.
  • The municipal benchmark curve shifted downward during the third quarter with rates falling by 19-36 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) across the curve, and the spread of 186 bps between 1-year and 30-year Municipal Market Data (MMD) tightened, down from 197 bps at the beginning of the quarter. We remain attentive to pockets of weakness or opportunity that may arise. With only 24 bps between 20-year and 30-year MMD, the continued compression makes extremely long-dated exposure less attractive for investors. In our view, the risk/reward trade-off strongly favors taking a more measured approach to curve allocations.

Quality—contributor

  • Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds over the period. The portfolio is underweight AA and AAA rated bonds, which underperformed, and overweight A and BBB bonds, which outperformed.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the July default by Brightline Florida demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—neutral

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the housing, hospital, industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR), and education sectors as well as underweight allocations to the state GO, electric, transportation, water, and sewer sectors. Detractors included overweight allocations to the special tax sector and underweights to the local GO and leasing sectors.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts along with essential services provided by municipalities are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the state GO, electric, IDR/PCR, and special tax sectors. Selection within the local GO, housing, transportation, water and sewer, and leasing sectors was negative.
  • The volatility observed in the first nine months of the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

A relatively short average maturity results in smaller price fluctuations in response to changes in market rates of interest. data.title: Arkema: Guidance Cut Priced In, Risk-Reward Turning More Attractive
data.description:
We revisit our investment case on Arkema (OTCPK:ARKAF) (OTCPK:ARKAY) after its Q3 results and a further downgrade to full-year guidance. Since our last update (H1 results), the company has shed roughly 15% of its market capitalization (Fig. 1). While lower guidance mechanically implies a lower intrinsic value, we remain forward-looking and continue to see the balance of risks and potential returns as strongly favourable.

For our new readers, we have good coverage of EU specialty chemical players, and Arkema is a diversified commodity and specialty chemicals company founded in 2004. This followed TotalEnergies' reorganisation of its chemicals portfolio. Despite a challenging market environment, our buy rating was supported by Arkema's higher-margin segment evolution, cost-saving measures, and a 15% discount to the company's 10-year median EV/EBITDA.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Upside

During the quarter, Arkema's sales fell 9% to €2.18 billion. The company mentioned the results of minus 4.7% compared to Q3 2024 at constant exchange rates. In numbers, prices, volume, acquisition, and FX reached -3.7%, -2.5%, +1.5%, and -3.8%, respectively (Fig. 2). The company reported Q3 EBITDA of €310 million, down 24% year on year. In Q3, free cash flow came in at €187 million, slightly above the €174 million recorded in Q3 2024. This result is supportive even considering the lower EBITDA year-on-year and higher non-recurring charges (-€22 million versus -€15 million), which were more than offset by a €94 million working-capital inflow (compared with a €6 million outflow in Q3 2024), lower tax payments (€25 million versus €52 million), and reduced OPEX (€131 million versus €167 million).

Source: Arkema Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

Divisionally (Fig. 3), in Adhesive Solutions, volumes fell 3% year-on-year, mainly due to softer demand in industrial adhesives and in the US, while prices slipped 1% year-on-year. This translated into EBITDA of €93 million, down 13% versus last year but 3% ahead of the €90 million consensus. The Q3 EBITDA margin was 13.8%, 190 basis points lower year-on-year. Advanced Materials delivered EBITDA of €152 million, a 20% year-on-year decline yet 1% above the €151 million Wall Street consensus estimates, reflecting a 4% drop in volumes and a 1% decline in prices. Also, in the Coating Solutions segment, EBITDA fell to €51 million, driven by weaker volumes and thin margins in acrylic acid. Lastly, the intermediates segment saw volumes up by 17%, but more than offset by a 22% reduction in prices. This led to an EBITDA of €38 million, down 26% year-on-year.

Fig 3

Why are we positive?

Management has revised its 2025 EBITDA guidance to a range of €1.25-1.30 billion (Fig. 4), from the previous indication of "around €1.3 billion." The midpoint of the new range is about 2% below the Visible Alpha consensus of €1.3 billion. To deliver full-year EBITDA of €1.275 billion, the implied Q4 EBITDA is roughly €270 million, which would represent a decline of around 17% year-on-year. Q3 EBITDA came in slightly ahead of Visible Alpha expectations. In our view, the implied Q4 run rate looks achievable, and after two consecutive guidance cuts, the probability of a further meaningful downgrade appears more limited. On the Q4 EBITDA, this would imply a slight sequential improvement. This is backed by likely lower input costs and favorable pricing.

Fig 4

Again, a core thesis in our buy rating reiteration in the H1 comment was supported by the adhesive solution, with higher margins and volumes. This was reiterated in the Q&A analyst call. In Q3, the core Specialty Materials business came in 3% ahead of consensus, which adds further supporting evidence.

Arkema's net debt, including €700 million of hybrid bonds, reached €3.4 billion, equivalent to 2.6x LTM EBITDA, down from €3.58 billion since H1 results. Q3 FCF was solid (Fig. 5), and even if the recurring FCF guidance was lowered to €300 million (from between €300 and €400 million), we do not expect consensus numbers to change significantly.

Fig 5

Despite softness in the US and persistently weak demand in Europe, which more than offset a more resilient performance in Asia, particularly China, Arkema's management team highlighted strong sales growth in batteries, sports, 3D printing, healthcare, and next-generation fluorospecialties (Fig. 6). This is something to consider in our investment thesis.

Fig 6

On our low cycle, Arkema shares are trading at 9x P/E and 5.4x EV/EBITDA. This is the lowest valuation within the sector despite some good niche growth positions. As we have often argued, the right time to buy chemicals is when valuations look optically expensive relative to depressed earnings and sentiment is washed out. The industry is in a cyclical trough. Arkema now sits squarely in this "perfect storm" and ticks all of those boxes. Therefore, we continue to see valuation upside ahead.

Valuation

As noted previously, a reduction in guidance naturally means a lower assessed valuation. Even in a depressed environment, Arkema achieved an EBITDA margin of 14.2%, FCF increased, and net debt decreased. Even assuming no 2026 recovery in volumes, we believe the group has its cost base firmly under control; on our estimates, this would support an unchanged dividend policy and allow for at least €150 million of deleveraging per year - upside that we have not reflected in our forecasts.

With the multiple unchanged at 7x on the low end of the company's guidance, our enterprise value reached €8.75 billion. Considering €3.3 billion in net debt (with Q4 FCF positive for €100 million), there is 18% upside from the current stock level, which is combined with a 6% dividend yield. Therefore, we see a total upside of almost 25% over the next 12 months. Additionally, Arkema still trades on a lower EV/EBITDA than the sector median (Fig. 7), so we do not include a multiple re-rating. Our target price is $82 per ADR (€70.8 per share).

Fig 7

Risks

Arkema's downside risks include a sustained slowdown in economic activity, especially in Europe, and lower spreads, which would decline margins. The company is also exposed to cyclical sectors, such as automotive. Additionally, as reported in the Q3 analysis, Arkema's risks include FX changes, particularly the US dollar, and raw material price volatility. Being a chemical player, the company's downside is stricter environmental regulations and litigation risks.

Conclusion

Even with lower guidance, we still see upside ahead. Arkema is a well-managed company with significant upside ahead (volume, growth in niche markets, margin expansion, deleveraging, etc.). With a valuation discount compared to peers, we remain buyers.

data.title: Broadcom: This AI Stock Is Set To Surge From Inference Demand
data.description:
The AI ecosystem's pivot from training to inference is now emerging as a strong revenue engine for hyperscalers, which is a structural tailwind for Broadcom's (AVGO)(AVGO:CA) custom silicon and networking products. This is key as the market is anxiously awaiting the moment that Big Tech will show ROI from capex spending, and according to Broadcom and others, that day may soon be arriving.

Broadcom joined Nvidia, Alphabet, and Microsoft in calling out surging AI inference demand, noting that this rapid growth could drive increased demand for custom silicon in the second half of 2026, and with it, higher AI revenue.

Broadcom is cementing itself as the clear second in AI with key ingredients for success as inference demand rises. With millions of users interacting frequently with AI assistants, inference becomes the focal point for providers such as OpenAI and Google. Meeting these levels of growing demand, without significant response delays or downtime, requires more accelerators, networking, and interconnect products.

Broadcom’s edge goes beyond the fact that custom accelerators are often multiples cheaper than Nvidia’s GPUs for inference tasks – it's that custom silicon is increasingly performant with each generation. By optimizing algorithms (software), Big Tech can drive higher performance from large language models (LLMs) -- which helps to drive down costs while also increasing output for specific workloads. For example, a rough idea as to how much it costs Nvidia to make merchant GPUs is estimated at around $3,000 to $5,000, whereas the company charges $25,000 to $30,000 – hence the AI leader’s excellent margins. Reducing Nvidia’s high pricing power is what Big Tech is after, and this can be accomplished both in terms of the hardware costs and also through optimizing the workloads for specific use cases.

Big Tech is prominent in Broadcom’s custom silicon customer list, which includes Google and Meta. ByteDance reportedly emerged as the third customer last summer, and the company also added an additional customer in the third quarter.

Why Big Tech Is Chasing Cheaper Inference

For the providers in the AI ecosystem, monetizing GPUs depends on inference, and thus revenue becomes a function of GPUs and tokens, and profits become a function of cost. Nvidia’s Blackwell offers a massive leap in performance and can train models such as Meta’s Llama 3.1 405B in as little as 27 minutes, yet the cost advantages offered by custom silicon can translate into higher margins in the long run from lower inference serving costs.

For example, Google announced that its upcoming seventh-gen TPU Ironwood is its “most performant and scalable custom AI accelerator to date, and the first designed specifically for inference.” Ironwood comes in two sizes, a 256- and a 9,216-chip configuration, with the larger size offering up to 42.5 exaflops of performance.

Google adds that Ironwood offers 2x the performance per watt as last year’s generation, Trillium, with 6x more HBM and 4.5x the HBM bandwidth. This allows it to deliver more capacity per watt at a time when power is a primary constraint and provide customers with more cost-effective AI workloads.

This is exactly what Broadcom sees arising from this inference growth curve, as CEO Hock Tan said during the Q2 earnings call that they might witness an acceleration of XPU demand into the back half of 2026 to meet urgent demand for inference on top of the demand for training. Most importantly, in the recent quarter the CEO highlighted that they added an additional customer and have secured over $10 billion in XPU orders. “And reflecting this, we now expect the outlook for our fiscal 2026 AI revenue to improve significantly from what we had indicated last quarter.”

The necessity of networking in larger clusters means demand is likely to remain robust even given custom silicon will not keep pace with Nvidia’s merchant sales into hundreds of billions.

Higher-than-expected deployments of custom silicon combined with strong demand for networking should provide robust tailwinds for AI revenue growth beyond 2026. Broadcom currently has enough visibility to place possible demand acceleration for 2H 2026 on the table, and this could easily persist through 2027 and beyond should inference demand flourish and as the path to 1 million accelerator clusters materializes.

Assuming Broadcom can maintain another over 60% YoY growth in FY27 on stronger demand and potential conversion of its three current prospects, AI revenue would close to about $54 billion, or about 50% share of revenue.

Tomahawk 6 Enabling Path to 1 Million Accelerator Clusters

Broadcom has been quite vocal about the industry’s path to 1-million-plus accelerator clusters, constantly reiterating how its three hyperscalers and now four “each race towards 1 million XPU clusters by the end of 2027.” This would be multiple times larger than current deployments, with xAI’s Colossus supercomputer expanding from 100K to 200K GPUs. Broadcom has continuously re-emphasized this forecast as it represents two major growth opportunities for the company: significant growth in accelerator deployments with inference tailwinds, and even more growth in networking deployments to support these clusters.

The shift to Ethernet and away from Nvidia’s lock-in ecosystem of GPU + InfiniBand is benefiting Broadcom, with the industry pointing to rising Ethernet demand. Arista said that momentum for Ethernet “has really shifted in the last year,” while Nvidia touted that its new Spectrum-X Ethernet is annualizing at $10 billion in revenue, or $2.5 billion quarterly.

The company is committed to remaining on the leading edge of networking with its Tomahawk 6 switch, the industry’s first 102.4 Tbps Ethernet switch. The next-gen switch doubled the bandwidth of its predecessor, while offering flexible deployment ability with 1,024 100G or 512 200G SerDes options, reducing switch count.

This raw performance upgrade paves the way for >100K to 1 million accelerator clusters by allowing larger leaf-spine fabrics to be constructed while drawing less power and keeping latency low. Broadcom exec Ram Velaga said that demand for the new switch is “unprecedented,” with multiple >100K accelerator deployments “using Tomahawk 6 for both the scale-out and scale-up interconnect.”

When discussing Tomahawk 6, management points toward the flattening of the AI cluster as an important catalyst for this product, stating:

[...] Tomahawk 6 enables clusters of more than 100,000 AI accelerators to be deployed in just two tiers instead of three ... this flattening of the AI cluster is huge because it enables much better performance in training next-generation frontier models through a lower latency, higher bandwidth and lower power.”

Additionally, in terms of the AI networking opportunity, scaling up is 5-10X more than scale out – setting up a nice trajectory as AI clusters grow. Read our previous coverage on AVGO here.

Q3 Revenue Grew by 22%

Q3 revenue was $15.95 billion, beating estimates for $15.82 billion and reflecting top-line growth of 22% YoY and 6.3% QoQ. This represents record quarterly revenue, driven by custom AI accelerators, networking switches, and VMware strength in Infrastructure Software. Looking ahead, management provided Q4 guidance of $17.4 billion in revenue, implying 23.8% YoY growth and an uptick to 9.1% QoQ growth. This guide suggests that AI strength and VMware remain stable contributors.

Analysts expect revenue to grow 22.7% in Q1 and will then accelerate to 25.5% in Q2 and 48.2% in Q3. Analysts expect strong FY2026 revenue growth of 34.9% YoY to $85.4 billion and 29.5% YoY to $110.6 billion in FY2027. Broadcom has a record backlog of $110 billion, and the management attributed the strong growth to AI demand.

Key Segments

Semiconductors were the main growth driver, fueled by AI accelerators and Ethernet networking. Infrastructure Software, which includes the VMware acquisition, remained strong even though growth decelerated slightly when compared to prior quarters.

Semiconductor Solutions Revenue accelerated nine points to 26% YoY at $9.17 billion, primarily driven by strong AI revenue growth. AI Semiconductor revenue surged 63% YoY to $5.2 billion, showing re-acceleration after a slower Q2 (+46% YoY). AI now represents 57% of Semiconductor revenue and 33% of total company revenue.

Management guided Q4 AI revenue to $6.2 billion, which would represent ~19% sequential growth and 68% YoY growth. The company reported eleven consecutive quarters of YoY growth. Though Broadcom did not lay out an FY25 AI revenue target, Q4 implies Broadcom is guiding $19.9 billion in AI revenue for the year, up 63% YoY from $12.2 billion in FY24.

Management also expects AI revenue for FY2026 to accelerate and be significantly higher than they had indicated earlier, after the addition of the new AI customer. The company aims to fulfill the new $10 billion AI order in the third quarter of FY2026.

Non-AI Semiconductor revenue remained flat at ~$4 billion, indicating continued end-market softness outside of AI. As seen below, the gap between AI and non-AI revenue is widening as AI growth accelerates. In Q4, driven by seasonality, management expects non-AI semiconductor revenue to grow low double digits sequentially to approximately $4.6 billion.

Infrastructure Software Revenue was $6.8 billion, ahead of guidance of $6.7 billion, primarily driven by strong bookings during the quarter. It was up $190 million from $6.6 billion in Q2 and up from $5.8 billion in the same period last year. Management expects infrastructure software revenue to be approximately $6.7 billion, up 15% YoY in the fourth quarter.

Operating Leverage Offsets Gross Margin Compression

Broadcom delivered balanced profitability in Q3 FY25. While AI hardware growth slightly compressed GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins, the company leveraged its scale and VMware’s recurring revenue to expand operating and net margins YoY. Broadcom’s ability to maintain a 67% adjusted EBITDA margin during a period of rapid AI expansion demonstrates exceptional cost control and pricing power, positioning it well for the Q4 acceleration.

GAAP gross margin of 67.1%, down 90 bps QoQ from 68% in Q2, and up from 63.9% in the same period last year. The margins were down sequentially due to the custom silicon mix, which typically carries lower margins. Broadcom is flexing its ability to manage cost structures even as AI accelerators scale rapidly.

Non-GAAP gross margin of 78.4%, down 100 bps QoQ from 79.4% in Q2 and up 100 bps YoY. This is driven by the increase in AI hardware revenue, which has lower gross margins compared to VMware’s high-margin software business.

GAAP operating margin of 36.9%, down 190 bps QoQ from 38.8% in Q2, but up significantly from 29% in the same period last year. QoQ decline reflects seasonal opex, and YoY improvement reflects strong operating leverage.

Adjusted net profits grew by 37.3% YoY to $8.4 billion, or 52.7% of revenue, compared to 46.8% in the same period last year.

Adjusted EBITDA was $10.7 billion, compared to $10 billion in Q2 and $8.2 billion in the same period last year. This represents an adjusted EBITDA margin of 67.1%, compared to 66.7% in Q2 and 62.9% in the same period last year.

EPS Growth Driven by Top-Line Growth & Economies of Scale

Non-GAAP EPS growth is the real story here: up 36.3% YoY, reflecting Broadcom’s ability to capitalize on AI strength while containing costs. GAAP EPS lagged slightly due to accounting for VMware’s amortization and elevated interest expenses – not driven by any core business weaknesses.

EPS growth of 36.3% outpaced revenue growth of 22%, highlighting the company’s dual-engine model: AI hardware scale drives top-line growth while VMware’s recurring software offsets margin volatility and lifts EPS. Looking forward, analysts expect adjusted EPS to grow 37.9% YoY to $9.31 in FY2026 and 31.5% YoY to $12.24 for FY2027.

Record Free Cash Flow

Broadcom converted 44% of revenue into free cash flow, placing it among the top-tier semiconductor companies for cash generation. Strong operating cash flow growth driven by AI semiconductor momentum and VMware’s software contribution. Broadcom continues to de-risk its balance sheet, reducing debt by $3 billion sequentially while building cash reserves and modestly increasing inventory ahead of anticipated Q4 AI revenue acceleration. With high free cash flow conversion and a strong balance sheet, Broadcom should have ample flexibility to return capital to shareholders and fund future AI growth initiatives, reinforcing its premium valuation. See below for a breakdown of cash flow figures:

  • Q3 operating cash flow of $7.2 billion, up 9.3% QoQ compared to $6.5 billion in Q2 and up 44.4% YoY. This represents an operating cash flow margin of 44.9%, up from 43.7% in Q2 and 38% in the same period last year.

  • Free cash flow grew by 46.6% YoY to $7.02 billion with a free cash flow margin of 44%, up 730 basis points YoY and up 130 basis points sequentially.

  • The company had cash of $10.7 billion, up from $9.5 billion in Q2. Debt was $64.2 billion, down from $67.3 billion in Q2.

  • Inventory of $2.18 billion, up from $2.11 billion. It was up 8% sequentially in anticipation of strong revenue growth next quarter.

Valuation

Broadcom currently trades at a forward P/S of 18.9x compared to Nvidia’s 21.4x. For most of this year, the company traded at a higher valuation than Nvidia due to the strong expected AI growth.

On the bottom line, Broadcom trades at 36.9x forward earnings, a 10% premium to Nvidia’s 40.4x. Broadcom has strong margins – 65.5% adjusted operating margin and 52.7% adjusted net margin – driving strong EPS growth, at a 35% expected CAGR through FY27. However, the custom silicon ramp presents some headwinds to gross margin as it grows its mix of share.

Broadcom’s competitiveness with Nvidia on margins and its ability to drive strong EPS growth via operating leverage, while capitalizing on growing accelerator and networking demand lend to its valuation, as it is a clear second to Nvidia and far ahead of smaller peers Marvell and AMD in AI revenue.

This premium suggests that the market is pricing in 70%+ AI revenue CAGR through FY27, above management’s current 60%. The implication here: Any sign of AI growth slowing or continued margin pressure could trigger a valuation reset.

Conclusion

The shift from AI training to AI inference is becoming increasingly visible as Big Tech and model providers highlight strong growth in tokens and revenue. Broadcom has already benefited from both increasing compute and networking needs – but we think the surge in inference demand will disproportionately (and positively) flow to Broadcom’s top line and bottom line.

This is because custom silicon’s cost advantages and ability to drive lower inference serving costs at scale create a strong value proposition for Big Tech. As more and larger clusters are deployed to serve exploding inference demand, there will be additional long-term tailwinds for networking for the Ethernet networking giant. With AI now making up a third of total revenue and Q4 revenue projected at $6.2 billion, Broadcom is slated to be a primary beneficiary of the inference era.

data.title: The India Fund, Inc. Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

Over the quarter, the Fund fell by 7.01%, underperforming the benchmark by 41 basis points. Negative stock selection effects in consumer discretionary and financials and the overweight to communication services were partially offset by the Fund's holdings in industrials, health care and energy.1

KFin Technologies was the top stock detractor over the period. As a Registrar and Transfer Agent business, it is seen as a proxy to the rise of the broader Indian stock market. While its results were broadly satisfactory, underpinned by robust performance in newer business segments and effective cost control, the share price was affected by a slowdown in the domestic mutual fund business. The Fund's private bank holdings detracted in August as concerns over tariffs and slowing GDP growth sparked fears of further rate cuts, which could squeeze margins.

Within the autos space, our holding in Mahindra & Mahindra lagged rivals but still outperformed the benchmark. Not holding Maruti Suzuki and Eicher Motors cost us relative performance as the stocks did well on the back of strong operational performance and volume growth. Auto systems supplier Uno Minda, on the other hand, was one of the top contributors to returns after reporting good results, including 16% year-on-year growth over the quarter, with encouraging contributions from both existing and new products. Elsewhere in the consumer discretionary sector, not holding Eternal, which owns the food delivery app Zomato, also weighed on returns as its share price did well, following better-than-expected results.

Meanwhile, information technology (IT) stocks came under pressure due to macro concerns as well as worries around the level of IT spending from clients, particularly in the US. Our small- and mid-cap holding in Coforge underperformed, as did Tata Consultancy Services (TTNQY) which further announced a 12,000-role job cut. Our real estate holdings also came under pressure due to near-term challenges around growth and we consolidated the Fund's exposure into the highest conviction names over the quarter.

On a positive note, our positions in infrastructure capex-related names in the capital goods sector did well. KEI Industries reported a 26% year-on-year revenue growth, most of it driven by volume as growth across companies in the cables and wires segment remained strong. Siemens Energy India also added to relative returns after reporting results that were in line with expectations. Order inflow was strong, reflecting robust demand across the company's transmission segment, and supporting the company's multi-year growth outlook. Overall, we expect the sector to remain a beneficiary of government spending on infrastructure, although at a moderating pace, with an eventual pick up in private spending.

The Fund's healthcare exposure also did well, with Global Health (Medanta) adding to relative returns. The specialty care hospital chain posted solid results where revenue exceeded market expectations due to robust volume-led growth. Profitability, however, fell slightly short due to doctor hires, annual increments and pre-operating losses at one of its businesses.

Market review

Indian equities declined over a rather challenging quarter, driven by several factors including US tariffs, persistent selling from foreign institutional investors and a relatively weaker rupee. While these were headwinds for the market, some of the prevailing negative sentiment was offset by a largely in-line earnings reporting season, relatively stable oil prices and the Indian government's goods and services tax (GST) reform announcement that supported performance in the consumer sectors, in particular. In this environment, the MSCI India Index fell 6.63% in US dollar terms.

US President Donald Trump imposed a 25% reciprocal duty on India in early August, followed by another 25% in response to India's continued oil purchases from Russia. In September, Trump further announced a US$100,000 hike in H-1B visa fees, which investors viewed negatively, but this was later clarified with more details. In addition, the US imposed 100% tariffs on patented and branded pharmaceutical imports, unless the manufacturer was already building domestic production capacity in the US. Elsewhere, Indian officials resumed their trade talks with the US in mid-September.

On the domestic front, India overhauled its GST framework, by reducing the number of tax slabs from four to two. This simplified structure coincided with the start of the festive season in the country. Coming at this time, these changes are expected to potentially boost consumer sentiment and spending, particularly in the discretionary categories.

Meanwhile, inflation in the country fell further, below the Reserve Bank of India's target band of 2% and 6%. The year-on-year consumer price index eased to 1.55% in July based on provisional data, the lowest level since June 2017, largely due to sharp decline in food prices. The central bank, for its part, kept the repo rate unchanged at 5.5%, as expected, during its August meeting. June quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth rate came in at 7.8%, beating market estimates, driven by a strong services sector.

Activity

We reintroduced Kotak Mahindra Bank, a full-service private-sector bank with stabilising asset quality. We also added Karur Vysya Bank, a solid regional bank with superior asset quality and exposure to fast-growing and high-yielding segments, and Bajaj Finance, a high-quality retail-focused NBFC that has been delivering superior returns.

We initiated a position in MakeMyTrip (MMYT), a direct play on India's booming online travel story. This is an aspirational consumption company that is seeing secular tailwinds driven by a young and rising middle class, increasing affordability of travel and improved connectivity both domestically and abroad. Meanwhile, online penetration is still growing as the market matures.

Conversely, we sold our position in Power Grid Corporation of India (PWGQY) to consolidate our power exposure into NTPC. We also exited Bajaj Auto, Godrej Properties, Havells India and Syngene International for better opportunities elsewhere.

Outlook & strategy

While India's long-term structural growth story remains intact, the country is facing some near-term uncertainties, including those brought on by US tariffs on Indian goods exported to the US.

The direct impact of those tariffs on India is expected to be relatively limited, given that approximately 80% of the Indian economy is domestically oriented. However, second-order effects could still materialise as a result of the broader global trade war. If the US macroeconomic environment weakens due to tariffs, corporate decisions, particularly around IT spending, may be affected, potentially impacting Indian IT services in the medium term.

In terms of portfolio positioning, we have relatively low exposure to Indian exporters directly affected by the tariffs, particularly those in the textiles and apparels sector. Most of our portfolio is focused on companies with domestic growth drivers, and given our quality focus, we expect the portfolio's downside to be well protected.

India is still expected to grow at a faster clip than peers such as China and other emerging markets. While earnings growth has slowed in recent quarters, we expect India to continue generating comfortable double-digit earnings growth over the medium term. In our view, India has the fiscal and monetary legroom to support the economy.

From a stock picking perspective, we are still finding pockets of good growth and quality across various sectors and sub-sectors. While market conditions remain fluid, we believe the Fund's emphasis on quality holdings may help mitigate downside risk and contribute to lower volatility over time. Our relatively defensive positions are well-placed should profit-taking emerge, and we believe any potential correction in their share prices could present attractive entry points, depending on broader market dynamics.

data.title: John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary 1 Highlights

  • The MSCI World Index, the fund's benchmark, produced a strong return in the third quarter.
  • The fund, while positive in absolute terms, didn't keep pace with the benchmark.
  • Dividend-paying stocks were generally out of favor at a time of heightened investor risk appetites.

Market review and outlook

Global equities rallied in the quarter, as a favorable combination of steady economic growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and waning concerns about tariffs proved highly supportive for equities. Dividend stocks generally lagged the broader indexes, however. Investors remained focused on more speculative areas of the market, as well as high-growth themes related to artificial intelligence.

The U.S. economy remained largely resilient, but signs of some weakness in the labor market have raised questions about the economic outlook. The geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrop has been challenging outside of the United States, with ongoing geopolitical tensions, broad deglobalization, and a realignment of global supply chains from tariffs and onshoring/reshoring threatening to suppress long-term growth. Given the uncertain macro environment and stretched valuations in the stock market, we believe the potential for elevated volatility is high. While we're cautiously optimistic on the path forward for the economy and equities from a fundamental standpoint, we think these factors indicate the need for vigilance and an emphasis on risk management.

We believe the fund is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resilience if growth begins to falter. We've sought to build a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and long-standing track records of sound capital allocation practices. We maintain conviction that these types of companies should have the ability to adapt and endure through potential disruptions. We believe diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against uncertainty and volatility.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's relative performance was pressured by an underweight position in the IT sector, particularly a zero weighting in Nvidia, Inc. (NVDA) The fund doesn't hold the stock because it doesn't offer an attractive dividend, but the stock continued to outperform the broader market. An underweight in Apple (AAPL), which recovered from its weakness in the first half of the year thanks to strong orders for the new iPhone, further detracted. On the other hand, a number of tech stocks—including Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. (TSM), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co., and Dell Technologies, Inc. (DELL)—were among the fund's top individual contributors for the quarter. Outside of technology, notable detractors included overweight positions in Philip Morris International, Inc. (PM), LyondellBasell Industries NV (LYB), and Texas Instruments, Inc.

On the positive side, the fund outperformed in healthcare thanks to strength in several pharmaceutical stocks. A position in AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), whose shares rose following a patent extension for a key drug, was the top contributor. Holdings in the Japanese company NHK Spring Co., Ltd. also contributed, as did the U.S. engine producer Cummins, Inc. (CMI)

Portfolio changes

We initiated new positions in Infrastruttura Wireless Italiane S.p.A. and Google parent Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), and we sold the fund's holdings in Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), Dow, Inc. (DOW), and Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB)
